data.title: To kick our social media habit, let’s start with the adults
data.description:
Feel strongly about these letters, or any other aspects of the news? Share your views by emailing us your Letter to the Editor at letters@scmp.com or filling in this Google form. Submissions should not exceed 400 words
I refer to the article, “Hong Kong should study social media impact on teens, craft its own policies: experts” (December 11).
It was a refreshing thought that excessive screen time poses a significant health concern, particularly among children. Several countries have in recent years implemented policies and proposals regarding smartphone usage restrictions and online gaming time limits. As early as 2021, for instance, China started to restrict minors’ online gaming time.
However, what about children learning habits from adults? I believe that kids learn from their surrounding environment and adapt to it easily.
While commuting from home to the office in Hong Kong, I see many adults of diverse ages engrossed in their phone or tablet without noticing who is beside them. From the timing of boarding the MTR to getting off, they are oblivious to their surroundings.
I saw an advertisement on social media highlighting the importance of regulating screen time limits on public transport. This will certainly help the environment revert to one that favours more human-centred connections while living in a techno-savvy world. However, this must be taught to children by adults around them, first at home and in public places.
When adults don’t practise moderation, how can the children follow? There is an urgent need to educate parents and adults about how their actions can motivate children to practise regulation in the home, school and public places.
Dr Anmol Mukhia, visiting assistant professor, Department of Politics and Public Administration, University of Hong Kong
No grounds for any UK concern over Jimmy Lai verdict
Your article, “Lai verdict seen as a test for London” (December 18), suggests that the reset of the Sino-British relationship could be derailed by the recent conviction of Jimmy Lai Chee-ying for serious national security offences. The article reflects views collected from China watchers divorced from reality.
The Sino-British Joint Declaration on the future of Hong Kong does not confer any responsibility on the government of the United Kingdom for Hong Kong after 1997, nor any right to interfere in Hong Kong’s judicial proceedings. The Joint Declaration consists of three declarations. The only declaration the UK government made is that “it will restore Hong Kong to the People’s Republic of China with effect from 1 July 1997”, while the Chinese government set out its basic policies regarding Hong Kong in its declaration and stated that these would be elaborated in an annex.
The UK government should differentiate the signal from the noise. According to recent polls, including surveys by the Office for National Statistics, the top concerns of the British people are the cost of living, the National Health Service and the economy. British leaders are well advised to prioritise fixing these problems rather than making a mountain out of a molehill.
The Jimmy Lai case has been hyped by self-interested campaigners for Lai’s release, human rights activists of all sorts and China haters bearing personal grudges.
On its part, the UK government has overhauled its national security laws and introduced new and comprehensive offences, as well as more stringent enforcement provisions in its National Security Act 2023. The act stresses balancing security and rights and limits access to civil legal aid services for individuals convicted of terrorism offences. The Criminal Justice Act 2003 allows non-jury trials for serious cases involving terrorism, national security or where there are circumstances making a jury trial impossible in the interest of justice. Major jury reforms were also introduced in 2025 to further restrict jury trials to tackle court backlogs.
Lai’s Next Media empire had been used as a platform for political mobilisation for such a long time, and Lai had been so deeply involved in anti-China politics that it would have been difficult to tell, in the process of jury selection, whether a juror harbours sympathy or antipathy against him.
Regina Ip, member, Hong Kong’s seventh-term Legislative Council
Congested roads inflict costs on Hong Kong’s economy
The Yau Ma Tei section of the Central Kowloon Bypass has opened, with Financial Secretary Paul Chan Mo-po heralding its boost to our city’s competitiveness.
Key infrastructure is certainly imperative for the economic future of Hong Kong but one cannot rejoice when Hong Kong streets remain blocked with illegally parked cars. Has the government calculated the loss in revenue and competitiveness this congestion causes?
Police enforcement is at best inadequate, with double yellow lines meaningless, double and triple parking a daily occurrence, pavements blocked and, most laughably, the red and white signs declaring “Vehicle waiting will be prosecuted without warning” surrounded by vehicles. The clogged arteries of Hong Kong are going to need more than a bypass to prevent a cardiac arrest to our competitiveness – and the quality of life of Hongkongers.
Mark Peaker, The Peak

data.title: ‘C-beauty’ brands find retail expansion outside China a slow slog
data.description:
With its signature fan-shaped wall of lipsticks displayed at the entrance, the Hong Kong store of Mao Geping Cosmetics at the Harbour City shopping complex in Tsim Sha Tsui appeared quiet on a recent weekday night.
That underscored a sluggish state of affairs at Mao Geping, about two months after opening its first overseas location in the city, and the broader challenges faced by Chinese beauty – dubbed C-beauty – brands seeking to expand sales outside the mainland.
The cosmetics firm was named after founder Mao Geping, China’s most famous make-up artist, who was behind numerous mainland films, television programmes and reality shows.
“Mao Geping is finally here in Hong Kong,” trumpeted the firm’s account on the Chinese social media platform RedNote, which only had 30 followers. This post received four heart emojis.
It was a lethargic showing for the Chinese cosmetics firm more than a year after it made a blistering trading debut in Hong Kong, where it raised HK$2.34 billion (US$300 million).
According to its prospectus, Mao Geping aimed to strengthen its “global brand presence and overseas market penetration”, using 15 per cent of its Hong Kong listing’s proceeds for that purpose. In a recent earnings call, the company told analysts that it had plans to expand into Singapore, Japan, France and the UK.

Despite the challenges in growing overseas, Hou Juncheng – the founder of Proya Cosmetics, China’s largest beauty products maker – said in a recent trip to Paris: “We don’t want to be just the Chinese Proya … We want to be the world’s Proya.”
At Proya’s annual general meeting in May, Hou said the company would pursue mergers and acquisitions overseas via its Paris subsidiary. The focus would be on sectors such as baby care, fragrance and men’s skincare, according to Chinese media reports.
Shanghai-listed Proya has filed for a secondary listing on the Hong Kong stock exchange by late October. The company planned to use part of the proceeds to establish research and development centres and overseas sales networks.
Compared to major Western cosmetics brands, “Chinese beauty brands are still relatively small in scale”, said Chris Gao, a consumer research analyst at CLSA in Hong Kong.
“For established domestic cosmetics companies attempting to expand abroad, progress is still in its early stages,” Gao said.
“To truly build brand power, you need offline visibility combined with online coordination. Offline penetration still takes time,” she added.
In the first six months of 2025, China’s total cosmetics exports reached 18.71 billion yuan (US$2.6 billion), up 12 per cent from a year earlier, with Southeast Asia as the major overseas market, according to a report from China Fortune Securities. It projected cosmetics exports to nearly double to 34.6 billion yuan by 2027.

While contract cosmetics manufacturers for global brands accounted for the bulk of those shipments, a growing number of home-grown Chinese brands have been venturing overseas, aided by marketing campaigns on social media platforms like TikTok.
According to CLSA’s Gao, some of these brands were created by Chinese contract manufacturers to specifically sell in overseas markets.
Similar to the strategy adopted by solar panel makers, electric vehicle manufacturers and on-demand delivery service providers because of fierce domestic competition, China’s beauty products makers have also explored overseas markets to drive growth.
Young cosmetics brands like Colorkey, Kans and Carslan have mainly used social media marketing to carve a niche in Southeast Asian countries.
Chinese make-up brand Florasis, which gained popularity on Chinese social media in 2019, opened in January its first international boutique in Tokyo, following its initial success selling through e-commerce platforms.

data.title: Indonesia’s Aceh residents traumatised by flooding – ‘worse than the tsunami’
data.description:
As Hamdaniyah Surbakti, a 62-year-old resident of Aceh province, began to pick up the pieces of her house that was destroyed by last month’s severe floods, she inevitably thought about the 2004 tsunami that devastated Indonesia and other parts of the world.
“It was worse than the 2004 tsunami,” said Hamdaniyah, referring to the recent torrential rains and flash floods that swept across northern Sumatra.
“When the tsunami came, it only hit one area around [the provincial capital of] Banda Aceh. The flood this time covered almost all of Aceh.”
On December 26, 2004, a 9.1 underground earthquake erupted off Sumatra, with the resultant tsunami killing more than 160,000 people in Aceh alone and levelling entire communities.
At the end of November, heavy rains brought by a rare equatorial cyclone battered North Sumatra, West Sumatra and Aceh, triggering flash floods and landslides that buried villages, cut off roads and displaced millions. More than 1,000 people were killed across Sumatra, according to authorities.
For survivors, the disaster felt more terrifying not because of the death toll but because of its scale and unpredictability.

The trauma has been compounded by slow and uneven recovery efforts, with families struggling to repair their homes, replace necessities and secure enough help to get by each day.
When floodwaters surged into Hamdaniyah’s home in the city of Langsa in Aceh, she was fearful for herself and her family. Her husband, Nurdin, who uses a wheelchair, climbed onto a bed while her sister, Hasbi Surbakti, was also trapped alongside the couple.
“Our neighbours said, ‘OK let’s try and see what we can do’ and hoisted us onto their backs,” Hamdaniyah told This Week in Asia.
When a nearby water catchment area burst, the flood became a torrent of churning mud and debris, causing the three of them to fall.
“I just shouted ‘Help, someone help me’ as I went under,” Nurdin recalled.
Nurdin, 71, said he was still traumatised by the incident, not because he was afraid of dying, but because he did not want to become a fatality far from home in such a dire situation.
“I wanted to die in the comfort of my own house, surrounded by my things,” he said, sobbing as he recounted his experience.


Nurdin and the two women were later carried to a local mosque, where they ate the only food available – a handful of dates. It was a full day later before they were given bread and pastries by residents who braved the floodwaters to distribute aid.
“When I left my home, I didn’t even have a 500 rupiah [3 US cents] coin with me. I fled with nothing but the clothes I was wearing,” Hamdaniyah said.
It would be eight days before the family could return to their house, which was by then filled with mud that reached up to their knee level. “We had to pay US$65 for someone to clear it, but they didn’t clean any of the furniture. We had to pay extra for that,” Hamdaniyah said.
In total, the family paid over US$150 to have their house cleaned and electronic appliances, including a fridge and a washing machine, fixed. They received a one-off payment of US$12 from the local government and 20kg (44 pounds) of rice, Hasbi said.
On Thursday, Murthalamuddin, a spokesman for emergency response in Aceh who uses a single name, said residents were lining the roads of the province and raising white flags as they begged for additional aid.
Suraiya, a senior adviser to Aceh Governor Muzakir Manaf, told Indonesian media on Saturday that the floods likely surpassed the 2004 tsunami in terms of the number of areas affected. “The people are extremely angry because we urgently need help. The problem is that while the central government is present, it can’t cover everything.”

In recent weeks, protests have taken place across Aceh, with residents urging the central government to declare a national emergency so that foreign aid can be sent to the province.
There has been speculation among residents that Jakarta’s slow disaster response in Aceh arose from the tense history between the two sides.
From the 1970s, Aceh was embroiled in a long and bloody civil war, which killed thousands of Acehnese and members of the Indonesian military as the Free Aceh Movement called for independence from the rest of Indonesia. In 2005, Aceh was granted special status as a semi-autonomous region of Indonesia.
“During that time, all you had to worry about was being shot, and then you would be dead. This was harder because we lived through it,” Nurdin said.
On Saturday, Indonesian President Prabowo Subianto said the central government could manage the disaster response and did not need foreign aid. Jakarta has deployed warships, helicopters and planes to drop food packages into isolated areas across Sumatra.
For Hamdaniyah, such efforts are scant consolation for residents, with the floods compounding their collective trauma from the tsunami and the civil war.
“I haven’t felt deep sadness like this since my daughter died,” she said.

Nurdin and Hamdaniyah’s 26-year-old daughter, Husna Clara, died suddenly due to health reasons in 2019, a week before she was due to be married.
“When the flood hit, I was sure I was going to die, and I thought ‘Well, it’s OK if it means that I can see my daughter again,’” she said.
Hamdaniyah has been visiting her daughter’s grave behind her home every day. She was horrified when she heard from neighbours that the graveyard where her daughter is buried was flooded.
“They said that six bodies – one was buried just three days before – floated up out of the ground and were carried away. The floodwaters tore the funeral shrouds of the deceased, and people could see their hair and faces.”
Hamdaniyah said while the bodies had been reburied, she was worried for a while that Clara could have been among them.
Despite their trying circumstances, Hamdanisyah said her family was “lucky” to have survived the flooding.
“Even though we don’t have much, we still have our house and each other. But I’m not hoping for anything in the future. My heart is broken by everything that has happened.”

data.title: Russell Brand charged with new counts of rape, sexual assault in UK
data.description:
UK police said two new charges, of rape and sexual assault, had been issued on Tuesday against British comedian and actor Russell Brand.
The media personality turned anti-establishment influencer already pleaded not guilty in May to five charges - one count of rape, one of oral rape, two of sexual assault and one of indecent assault between 1999 and 2005.
“These new charges are in relation to two further women and are in addition to the charges issued to Brand in April 2025 which involved four women,” the Metropolitan Police said in a statement.
It stressed the total charges now related to six women after further police investigations.

Brand, 50, the ex-husband of US singer Katy Perry, has been summoned to appear at Westminster Magistrates’ Court on January 20 to face the two new charges.
A trial is set to begin at Southwark Crown Court on June 16 on the five original charges.
On X, after the new charges against him were announced, Brand said he felt “blessed” to “have the opportunity to atone for the many things over the years I did wrong” and for being able “to ensure that people understand the truth of my situation”.
He said: “I pray Lord that anyone that I’ve harmed or hurt in my years of mindlessness in sin would be healed”.
Prosecutors charged Brand following a police probe into allegations aired in a Channel 4 documentary.
He is accused of raping one woman in a hotel room following an event in the southern Bournemouth area in 1999.
Another charge relates to the oral rape and sexual assault of a woman in 2004 in central London.
After those initial charges, Brand posted a video to social media saying “I never was a rapist. I’ve never engaged in non-consensual activity”.
Detective Chief Inspector Tariq Farooqi said that the women who have reported Brand’s alleged behaviour “continue to receive support from specially trained officers”.
He added the police investigation was ongoing and urged “anyone affected by this case, or anyone with information, to come forward”.
Born in 1975 to working-class parents in Essex, east of London, Brand began his stand-up career as a teenager, eventually working as an MTV presenter and host of a Big Brother spin-off.
He presented a show on the BBC’s Radio 2 station between 2006 and 2008, but quit after an on-air prank when he left a sexually explicit voicemail for Fawlty Towers actor Andrew Sachs about his granddaughter.
Once a left-leaning political campaigner and Hollywood star, he has rebranded himself as a conservative guru to his millions of social media followers.
Last year, he said he became a Christian after being baptised in the Thames river.
Brand most recently appeared at Turning Point USA’s AmericaFest 2025.

data.title: US hits 5 Europeans with visa bans in ‘censorship’ row
data.description:
The US State Department announced on Tuesday it was barring five Europeans it accused of leading efforts to pressure US tech firms to censor or suppress American viewpoints.
The Europeans, characterised by US Secretary of State Marco Rubio as “radical” activists and “weaponised” non-governmental organisations, fell afoul of a new visa policy announced in May to restrict the entry of foreigners deemed responsible for censorship of protected speech in the United States.
“For far too long, ideologues in Europe have led organized efforts to coerce American platforms to punish American viewpoints they oppose,” Rubio posted on X. “The Trump Administration will no longer tolerate these egregious acts of extraterritorial censorship.”
The five Europeans were identified by Sarah Rogers, the undersecretary of state for public diplomacy, in a series of posts on social media. They include the leaders of organisations that address digital hate and a former European Union commissioner who clashed with tech billionaire Elon Musk over broadcasting an online interview with Donald Trump.
Rubio’s statement said they advanced foreign government censorship campaigns against Americans and US companies, which he said created “potentially serious adverse foreign policy consequences” for the US.
The action to bar them from the US is part of a Trump administration campaign against foreign influence over online speech, using immigration law rather than platform regulations or sanctions.

The five Europeans named by Rogers are: Imran Ahmed, chief executive of the Centre for Countering Digital Hate; Josephine Ballon and Anna-Lena von Hodenberg, leaders of HateAid, a German organisation; Clare Melford, who runs the Global Disinformation Index; and former EU commissioner Thierry Breton, who was responsible for digital affairs.
Rogers in her post on X called Breton, a French business executive and former finance minister, the “mastermind” behind the EU’s Digital Services Act, which imposes a set of strict requirements designed to keep internet users safe online. This includes flagging harmful or illegal content like hate speech.
She referred to Breton warning Musk of a possible “amplification of harmful content” by broadcasting his livestream interview with Trump in August 2024 when he was running for president.
Breton responded Tuesday on X by noting that all 27 EU members voted for the Digital Services Act in 2022. “To our American friends: ‘Censorship isn’t where you think it is,’” he wrote.
French Foreign Minister Jean-Noel Barrot said France condemned the visa restrictions on Breton and the four others. Also posting on X, he said the DSA was adopted to ensure that “what is illegal offline is also illegal online”. He said it “has absolutely no extraterritorial reach and in no way concerns the United States”.
Most Europeans are covered by the Visa Waiver Programme, which means they don’t necessarily need visas to come into the country. They do, however, need to complete an online application prior to arrival under a system run by the Department of Homeland Security, so it is possible that at least some of these five people have been flagged to DHS, a US official said, speaking on condition of anonymity to discuss details not publicly released.
Other visa restriction policies were announced this year, along with bans targeting foreign visitors from certain African and Middle Eastern countries and the Palestinian Authority. Visitors from some countries could be required to post a financial bond when applying for a visa.

data.title: Pentagon says China’s ‘historic’ military build-up has made US ‘increasingly vulnerable’
data.description:
Washington is not pursuing a forceful strategy towards China but Beijing’s “historic” military build-up is making the US increasingly vulnerable, the Pentagon said in its annual report to Congress on military developments related to China.
“We do not seek to strangle, dominate, or humiliate China … We seek only to deny the ability of any country in the Indo-Pacific to dominate us or our allies,” the 100-page report released on Tuesday said.
“China’s historic military build-up has made the US homeland increasingly vulnerable” it added, citing Beijing’s growing arsenal of nuclear, maritime and conventional long-range strike, cyber and space capabilities “able to directly threaten Americans’ security”.
The readout, which dropped late in the day during a holiday week, argued that America’s goal is to be so strong that aggression by its adversaries is not even considered, “and that peace is therefore preferred and preserved”.
In keeping with an oft-repeated theme by the administration of US President Donald Trump, the report argued that US-China military relations had rarely been in better shape than under Washington’s current leadership.
“Under President Trump’s leadership, relations between the United States and China are stronger than they have been in many years,” the report said, adding that the Pentagon would “support efforts” to build on that momentum with the People’s Liberation Army.
“We will do so in part by opening a wider range of military-to-military communications with the PLA with a focus on strategic stability as well as de-confliction and de-escalation, more broadly. We will also seek other ways to make clear our peaceful intentions.”
The report points to China’s growing capability as a cause for concern and potential instability. It touts Beijing’s foremost military strategy as overcoming the US through “national total war”, using a whole-of-nation mobilisation campaign.
Titled “Military and Security Developments Involving the People’s Republic of China”, the report said the Chinese military continued to make steady progress towards three 2027 goals: to be able to achieve “strategic decisive victory” over Taiwan; to gain “strategic counterbalance” against the US in nuclear and other strategic domains; and “strategic deterrence and control” against other regional countries.
Beijing sees Taiwan as part of China to be reunited by force if necessary. Most countries, including the United States, do not recognise Taiwan as an independent state, but Washington is opposed to any attempt to take the self-ruled island by force and is committed to supplying it with weapons.
While the annual report notes that Beijing and Moscow continue to deepen their ties, likely “driven by a shared interest in countering the United States”, it says that cooperation is undercut by their mutual distrust of each other.
The Tuesday report came a few weeks after the release of the US National Security Strategy – also dropped without much fanfare on a Friday – which was less focused on China than on domestic issues and what the administration has termed the “Trump Corollary to the Monroe Doctrine” that emphasises the strategic importance of the western hemisphere.


Analysts said Tuesday’s report appeared to be put together quickly. It lacks a table of contents, and some sections seemed to be dated. It cites, for instance, tepid relations between China and North Korea “with minimal high-level exchanges”, without mentioning that North Korean leader Kim Jong-un featured prominently at China’s showcase military parade in early September.
That said, the report provides insight into how the Pentagon and the White House see bilateral relations, according to analysts.
“The tone is notably constructive towards China, stressing that bilateral relations are ‘stronger’ than they have been in years,” said Jeremy Chan, senior analyst with the Eurasia Group.
“While this claim is dubious, it is also a revealing signal of how Trump views his policy towards China and his reluctance to press any claims with Beijing that might disrupt the detente reached in Busan in October.”
The report recognises China’s military ambitions and steady progress but does not attempt to project too far into the future, added Chan, a former official in the US consulate in Shenyang, Liaoning province.
“It is only a matter of time until China’s power projection goes global,” he said. “This recognition of China’s expanding military capabilities will have potentially far reaching implications for US defence commitments in the western Pacific.”

data.title: Hong Kong stocks rise as Wall Street rally boosts confidence in earnings growth
data.description:
Hong Kong stocks rose on Wednesday, tracking Wall Street’s rally, as investors grew more confident about earnings growth prospects despite fading expectations for near-term US rate cuts.
The Hang Seng Index increased 0.2 per cent to 25,812.34 at 9.50am local time, erasing the 0.1 per cent loss recorded on Tuesday. The Hang Seng Tech Index climbed 0.4 per cent. On the mainland, the CSI 300 Index and the Shanghai Composite Index were little changed.
Hong Kong’s stock market will trade for only half a day on Wednesday and remain closed on Thursday and Friday for the Christmas holiday.
Leading the gainers, search-engine giant Baidu jumped 1.5 per cent to HK$121.60 and online-game provider NetEase advanced 1 per cent to HK$214.80. Home-grown chipmaker SMIC surged 4.7 per cent to HK$72.15, and smartphone and carmaker Xiaomi added 0.5 per cent to HK$214.40.
Limiting the advance, travel-booking agency Trip.com retreated 0.4 per cent to HK$565.50 and power-tools producer Techtronic Industries fell 0.3 per cent to HK$91.40. Sportswear maker Li Ning dropped 1.3 per cent to HK$19 and peer Anta Sports Products slipped 0.9 per cent to HK$81.55. Short-video platform Kuaishou Technology slid 0.4 per cent to HK$64.10, and e-commerce firm Alibaba Group Holding fell 0.5 per cent to HK$146.50.
Overnight in the US, the S&P Index rose for a fourth day to close 0.5 per cent higher and the Nasdaq climbed 0.6 per cent, as risk appetite improved ahead of the Christmas holiday. Investors brushed off reduced expectations for near-term Fed easing after strong economic data, betting instead on resilient earnings growth into 2026.
One stock debuted today. Ningbo Jansen Superconducting Technologies jumped 257 per cent to 66.27 yuan in Shanghai.
Elsewhere in Asia-Pacific, Japan’s Nikkei 225 lifted 0.3 per cent, and South Korea’s Kospi rose 0.2 per cent, while Australia’s S&P/ASX 200 fell 0.4 per cent.

data.title: Operation Santa Claus: Hong Kong shopping centre brings cheer with ‘Ticket to Christmas’
data.description:
A shopping centre’s imaginary world, featuring a festive train station inhabited by colourful animal characters, is adding holiday cheer for shoppers while supporting Hong Kong’s annual fundraising campaign, Operation Santa Claus (OSC).
“Ticket to Christmas” is the theme of this year’s celebrations at Admiralty’s Pacific Place and the nearby Starstreet Precinct – a Wan Chai area comprising shops, bars, cafes and restaurants connected to the centre by the Three Pacific Place underground link.
The precinct covers Wing Fung Street, Star Street, Moon Street, Sun Street and St Francis Yard.
Shoppers can visit the centre’s inventive Christmas Grand Central attraction, staffed by station master Clarence the Crocodile, train captain Bernie the Father Bear, and parcel-carrying George the Giraffe.

Ellie the Elephant sits atop the building, ringing a bell, while the train’s passengers include Professor Hugo the hippopotamus and his wife, Harper, giant carrot-carrying farmer Remi the Rabbit and the cute Kody the Koala.
Wing Chiu, general manager of Pacific Place, which is owned and operated by Swire Properties, said: “The mall’s initiative invites the community to come together, reconnect and share in the spirit of the festive season.”
The attractions, running until January 2, include an immersive animated train ride across continents, a Christmas Day musical “Whistle-Stop” show, shopping rewards and the sale of merchandise in the station’s gift shop, Wonderbox.
All proceeds from “Santa Express” – a meet-and-greet printed photo session with Santa Claus, priced between HK$220 and HK$280 (US$28 and US$36) per ticket – are being donated directly to OSC.

Three complimentary Christmas-themed handicraft workshops – “Stained Glass Timepiece”, “Festive Cake Candle” and “Christmas Wreath” – have also been held at the Station Cafe.
Those attending the workshops were selected from members of two OSC beneficiaries. One group was Uplifters, a charity supporting domestic workers mainly from the Philippines and Indonesia, while eight participants were chosen from another OSC beneficiary, the Hong Kong Workers’ Health Centre, which assists people who have suffered work-related musculoskeletal disorders.
Pacific Place has been a dedicated supporter of OSC since 2013.
“The partnership with OSC reflects Swire Properties’ deep commitment to placemaking and care for the community,” Chiu said.
“The aim is to leverage the reach and influence of Pacific Place to raise awareness and generate donations. To help foster social inclusion, the beneficiaries are invited to participate directly in the workshops, which are designed to be hands-on and uplifting, while offering participants a chance to express creativity and take home a festive keepsake.”
After the festive season ends, the centre’s giant baubles, figurines and decorations will not be packed away in storage this year. Instead, they are being sold to raise extra funds for charity.

Pacific Place will put its many festive decorations up for sale on the OSC eShop in mid-January so members of the public can buy them to decorate their own homes in future while knowing that their purchases have helped to support OSC’s worthy causes.
Pacific Place said that, in addition to raising funds for OSC, it was also supporting Swire Group’s disaster relief efforts by donating all of the proceeds from the sale of its “Ticket to Christmas” merchandise during the festive season to an NGO’s fund that provides support for victims affected by last month’s Tai Po inferno that claimed at least 161 lives and displaced around 5,000 people.

OSC, jointly established by the Post and public broadcaster RTHK in 1988, has raised around HK$396 million for more than 365 charity projects and helped more than 100,000 people throughout the years.
In addition to its annual fundraising drive, it is leading an emergency appeal to meet the specific needs of residents affected by the inferno.
All proceeds will support this year’s 13 OSC-funded charity projects and community partners involved in the fire relief efforts in Tai Po.
Click here to learn more about how to donate to OSC.

data.title: Brooks Koepka quits LIV Golf for family reasons, Gooch to captain Smash GC in 2026
data.description:
Brooks Koepka, one of the LIV Golf originals, has quit the league ahead of the 2026 season, opening the door for a potential return to the PGA Tour.
The five-time major winner said he was leaving for family reasons in a statement released on Tuesday.
LIV Golf said the split was “amicably and mutually agreed”, adding that Talor Gooch would become the new captain of Smash GC, who Koepka had led since joining the breakaway tour in 2022.
“Brooks is prioritising the needs of his family and staying closer to home,” Scott O’Neil, the LIV Golf CEO, said. “We appreciate the significant impact he has had on the game and wish him continued success, both on and off the course.”
Koepka’s decision comes after his wife, Jena Sims, revealed in October she had suffered a miscarriage 16 weeks into her pregnancy.

Koepka’s tenure with LIV was laden with highs and lows. When he won the 2023 PGA Championship after spending most of his days playing ‍in LIV’s comparatively non-competitive environment that featured 54-hole, no-cut tournaments, it gave LIV a big boost of credibility.
On the ‍flip side, Koepka occasionally expressed his frustration with his situation – sounding like the 2017 and 2018 US Open champion and 2018, 2019, 2023 PGA titlist was marking time.
“I’ve ‌got a contract obligation out here to fulfil, and then we’ll see what happens,” Koepka said at one LIV ‍event.
His official third-person statement did not offer any hints as to his future.
“Brooks Koepka will be stepping away from LIV Golf. He is deeply grateful to Yasir Al-Rumayyan, Scott O’Neil, and the LIV Golf leadership team, his teammates, and the fans.
“Family has always guided ‌Brooks’s decisions, ⁠and he feels this is the right moment to spend more time at home. Brooks will continue to be a huge supporter of LIV Golf and wishes the league and its players continued success. Brooks remains passionate about the game of golf and will keep fans updated on what’s ahead.”
While Koepka can tee ‌it up for all four majors next year thanks to his 2023 PGA Championship victory, the PGA Tour requires LIV exiles to sit out one year from their last LIV tournament. His ‌last appearance was on August 24 ‌at the LIV Golf Michigan event.
In the 35-year-old’s absence, Gooch will take over as captain of Smash, and said he was “honoured and excited” by the opportunity.
O’Neil, meanwhile, called Gooch, who has won several times on the tour and was the 2023 individual champion, “a world-class talent ready for this leadership role”.
“This moment reinforces the healthier, more flexible global golf ecosystem that we’re striving to create,” O’Neil added. “One that supports players’ rights to compete on their terms while also showcasing our league’s competitive depth and the readiness of our players to step into leadership roles.”

data.title: China is now the good guy in climate change
data.description:
China draws flak in the West for seemingly everything it does. However, it’s the end of the year and we shouldn’t be so down on China, at least not all the time. So let me draw attention to a major development in the country that’s making the world a better place: clean energy.
That is a major “China shock” if ever there was one, but no one is putting it that way. Critics like to cite the stats that China burns as much coal as the rest of the world combined. That is true, but the country burns it to produce energy mostly to make the things the rest of the world buys cheaply.
In any case, China is so committed to weaning itself off coal that in the process it is creating world-beating technologies and industries in clean energy. This epochal energy transition is pushing many other countries to move away from coal, gas and fossil fuels, especially those in the developing world.
That is according to a recent report by the British clean energy think tank Ember. The non-profit research group concludes that China’s transition is “fundamentally reshaping the economics of energy across the world” by making clean energy and its infrastructure affordable to developing economies, not only within China.
The country is moving towards a peak in energy-related fossil fuel use by accelerating investments in renewables, grids and storage, along with the electrification of transport, buildings and industry.
Consider some stats cited by Ember. China is the world’s biggest investor in clean energy, spending US$625 billion in 2024, or almost a third of the global total of US$2.03 trillion. The volume of installed battery storage tripled in the three years to 2024. Grid investment rose to an all-time high in 2024 of 608 billion yuan (US$86.3 billion), up 25 per cent from 486 billion yuan in 2019.


Last year, investment and production in clean energy contributed 13.6 trillion yuan to the national economy, equivalent to about one-tenth of its GDP, or roughly the total GDP of Australia. The sector is growing three times faster than the Chinese economy overall.
How does China do it? This is state-led industrial policy at its best. Continuing the momentum built up across a decade, the latest 15th five-year plan – which runs until 2030 – seeks to replace energy from fossil fuels with renewables under the goals set by the Paris Agreement with the “dual carbon” tasks of reaching peak emissions by 2030 and carbon neutrality by 2060.
“These investments in the clean energy future are driving dramatic cost reductions across the world in key technologies such as wind turbines, solar panels, storage batteries and electric vehicles,” according to Ember.


By doing so, the country is also reducing costs and accelerating the uptake of clean-energy-powered technologies in other countries. “The benefits are increasingly being felt in emerging markets, many of which are overtaking OECD countries in wind and solar generation share and in electrification,” the Ember report said.
The prices of these technologies are down by 60 to 90 per cent from 2010. By 2024, the report said, more than 90 per cent of wind and solar projects commissioned worldwide produced energy more cheaply than the cheapest fossil fuel sources. Cheaper renewables for everyone are helping to pave the way for fossil fuel use globally “to peak and decline”, according to the Ember report.
Meanwhile, the United States is doing the opposite. The administration of US President Donald Trump has moved to end federal support for renewables in industry and research, and it is pressuring other countries to buy American fossil fuels in trade negotiations under threats of high tariffs. It is promoting the domestic oil and gas industry, including encouraging drilling in the Arctic. Trump has embraced the famous mantra of “drill, baby, drill”.

Chinese companies now supply about 80 per cent of solar panels and 60 per cent of wind turbines worldwide, according to Ember. Not only do US companies struggle to compete, they are actively discouraged by their own government from committing to renewables.
Maybe renewables don’t promote American democracy and free-market capitalism. However, they do help developing economies build wealth and better health for citizens, a different but essential kind of freedom.
It’s curious that the US government is run by a political party that denies – or at least plays down – climate change and is forcing the world to buy its oil and gas. Meanwhile, authoritarian China is helping the world adopt renewables.
